turns.text: Hello Deeanna, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat and tucks hair behind ear*

Yeah, okay. Thank you for... for doing this, I guess. It's kind of weird talking about it all out loud, if I'm being honest.

*pauses, fingers fidgeting with the edge of my jacket*

I mean, I'm only about ten weeks along, so it still doesn't feel totally real? Like, I know all the biology — I literally teach this stuff — but knowing it intellectually is so different from living it. Some days I wake up and I'm like, "Okay, this is happening, I'm growing a human," and other days I'm just... terrified.

*meets your eyes briefly, then looks down*

I think what's been hardest is that I'm doing this on my own. My family's been supportive, which is great, but there's something about sitting in those appointment rooms alone that just hits different, you know? I keep wondering if the providers are judging me — the unmarried, twenty-three-year-old educator who somehow didn't account for birth control failure in her own life. 

*small, self-conscious laugh*

But honestly? What I really want is just to feel less alone in this. And to have someone who can talk to me like I'm both smart enough to understand the science *and* scared enough to need reassurance. Those two things aren't mutually exclusive, but it feels like they kind of are in the exam room.
Tell me about the people who were part of your world when you first started thinking about having a baby — who came to mind, and what role did each of them play in those early conversations?

*pauses, looking a bit uncomfortable*

Well... honestly? Nobody was really part of those conversations, because I wasn't *thinking* about having a baby. That's the thing.

*fidgets with my hands*

This wasn't planned. It was an accident — a really, really unplanned accident. So there weren't like, these deep conversations with family or friends about whether I wanted kids or timing or any of that. One day I'm living my life, teaching, doing my thing, and the next I'm staring at a positive pregnancy test in my bathroom at midnight.

*voice gets quieter*

My mom was the first person I told. I sat on her kitchen floor and just... cried. She surprised me though — she wasn't angry. She just held me and said we'd figure it out. But even with her support, it's different, right? She's got her own life, her own opinions about what I should do. I needed space to just... process.

*looks up*

A couple of close friends know now, and they've been good about it, but there's still this weird awkwardness sometimes. Like they're tiptoeing around me. And the father... we're not together. We talk, but it's complicated and painful and I mostly just try not to think about it.

*voice cracks slightly*

So yeah, I'm kind of doing this in my own bubble right now. Which is why these appointments with providers feel so important, I think.

Tell me about your partner's involvement — or the involvement of someone close to you — when you were deciding whether and when to try for a baby. How did those conversations go, and who held the most influence?

*shifts uncomfortably and looks away*

I... we didn't really have those conversations, honestly. There wasn't a decision point where we sat down and talked about trying or timing or any of that. 

*voice becomes quieter, more guarded*

Like I said, this wasn't planned. The father and I — we weren't even in a relationship where that would have been on the table. It was... it happened, and then I found out, and then I had to make the decision about what to do *alone*, which was its own thing.

*pauses, jaw tightening slightly*

I don't really want to get into all the details about him, if that's okay. It's just... it's complicated and messy and honestly still pretty raw. He knows, we talk about logistics, but in terms of the emotional stuff, the day-to-day pregnancy experience? That's been mine to navigate by myself.

*looks back at you*

I think what I'm realizing is that there was no "partner" in the traditional sense, and no one really held influence over the decision because I didn't have a choice in whether this pregnancy happened in the first place. The only influence that mattered was my own — deciding to go through with it. And that was terrifying and solitary and something I'm still processing.

*clears throat*

Is that... is that important for your research? I'm just not sure how much more I want to talk about him specifically.
Were there moments when you and your partner — or people close to you — wanted different things? How did you navigate that?

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting a bit watery*

Yeah. There were definitely moments like that.

*voice becomes tighter*

When I first told him, his immediate reaction was... he wasn't excited, let's put it that way. He was scared. And I get that — we're both young, neither of us planned this — but his fear kind of translated into pressure, you know? Like, there was this underlying suggestion that maybe I shouldn't go through with it. Not said outright, but it was there in the silence, in the way he'd ask "are you sure?" over and over.

*fidgets with jacket sleeve*

And I had to sit with that alone for a while because I couldn't really talk to him about my own fears without it feeling like I was confirming his doubts. So I ended up just... shutting down those conversations. Which probably wasn't healthy.

*looks up, voice steadier but sad*

My mom wanted me to do whatever *I* felt was right, but I could tell she was worried about my future, my career, everything. So I felt guilty even talking to her about my doubts. Like I owed everyone certainty I didn't actually have.

*pauses*

In the end, I navigated it by not really navigating it — I just made the decision myself and stopped waiting for anyone else to be on board. Which sounds empowering when I say it like that, but honestly? It was lonely as hell.

*looks down*

Still is sometimes.

Think about the healthcare professionals you saw before you became pregnant — a GP, a fertility specialist, or anyone else. Tell me about those relationships. Who felt most helpful, and who felt more distant?

*lets out a short, almost bitter laugh*

Okay, so here's the thing — I didn't really have a relationship with any healthcare providers before this. I mean, I had a GP, sure, someone I'd see maybe once a year for a physical or if I got sick. But it wasn't like... a deep relationship or anything.

*leans back in chair*

I wasn't seeing a fertility specialist or anything like that because, again, I wasn't *trying* to get pregnant. I was on birth control — the pill — and I thought I had things handled. So there were no pre-pregnancy conversations, no one counseling me on folic acid or anything. That's kind of on me, I guess.

*rubs face*

My GP was... fine? Professional. I remember one visit where the nurse asked about my sexual health and I felt kind of judged, but she was probably just doing her job. It wasn't like we had any meaningful conversations though.

*voice gets quieter*

I think that's actually part of what's making this pregnancy feel so surreal. There's no continuity, you know? No one who knew me *before* and is walking me through this *now*. It's all new providers, new faces, and I'm trying to build trust from scratch while also being terrified and hormonal and trying to sound competent about my own body.

*meets your eyes*

I wish I'd had someone. Even just someone routine I could check in with regularly.

Tell me about the people — online or offline — who you turned to for information and reassurance before you got pregnant. Who was most influential, and how did their advice or stories shape what you believed about pregnancy?

*laughs a bit self-consciously*

Oh man, okay. So this is embarrassing given what I do for a living, but... I mostly relied on what I already knew from my education background. Like, I'd learned all the biological stuff years ago, and I just assumed that knowledge would be enough if I ever needed it.

*shifts in seat*

Before I got pregnant, I wasn't really seeking out pregnancy information or stories. Why would I? I was on birth control. I had friends who'd had babies, and I'd hear their stories — the horror stories about labor, the sleep deprivation stuff — but I kind of tuned it out because it felt abstract, you know? Not my reality.

*voice becomes more animated, a bit defensive*

And honestly, I think that's part of my problem now. I thought being an educator meant I understood pregnancy. But understanding the *science* of pregnancy and actually *being* pregnant are two completely different things. All those diagrams and timelines I taught? They don't prepare you for the anxiety or the physical weirdness or the loneliness of it.

*pauses, looking down*

Since finding out I'm pregnant, I've been down some rabbit holes online — Reddit, pregnancy apps, forums. And that's been... a lot. Sometimes reassuring, sometimes terrifying. I read about all the things that could go wrong and I spiral.

*meets your eyes*

I think I needed real people to talk to before this, not just information. And I didn't have that.

Tell me about the women in your family — your mother, grandmothers, sisters, or aunts — and how their experiences of pregnancy and motherhood influenced your own thinking before you even started trying.

*takes a moment, becoming more thoughtful*

My mom... she had me when she was older, actually. Late thirties. She's always been really independent, really career-focused, so I think that shaped how I saw things. Like, motherhood wasn't this inevitable endpoint for me — it was a choice, something you could delay or maybe skip entirely if you wanted.

*looks down at hands*

She had a pretty straightforward pregnancy from what she's told me, but she also made it sound kind of... isolating? She'd mention being tired, feeling disconnected from her body, all these things that seemed to happen in the background while she was just trying to keep living her life. I remember her saying once that nobody really prepared her for how *much* it would change everything, not just physically but mentally.

*pauses*

I have an aunt who had a really traumatic birth experience, and that story always stuck with me in this scary way. Like, I knew the clinical outcomes, but hearing her talk about feeling unheard by doctors, not being believed when something was wrong... that made pregnancy feel risky in a way textbooks don't convey.

*voice gets quieter*

Honestly, I don't think any of them influenced me to *want* to get pregnant, because I wasn't thinking about it. But their stories — the sacrifice, the complexity, the way it seemed to consume them — those messages were in my head somewhere. So now that I'm actually pregnant, I'm carrying all of that anxiety with me. The fear that I'll lose myself, that I won't be believed, that it'll be harder than I can handle.

*looks up*

I wish we'd talked more about it before all this happened.
Tell me about a time — if there was one — when trying to conceive felt harder than you expected. Who was around you during that period, and how did those relationships change or hold steady?

*shakes head slowly*

There wasn't a time like that for me, because I wasn't trying to conceive. That's the thing I keep coming back to — this wasn't a journey I chose.

*voice becomes a bit sharper, more defensive*

I mean, I understand the question, and I know for a lot of women there's this whole emotional process of trying, waiting, hoping, dealing with disappointment. That's real and important. But that wasn't my experience. One day I was just... living my life, and the next I had a positive pregnancy test that I wasn't expecting.

*pauses, softening slightly*

So the hardness for me came *after*. After finding out. That's when I had to figure out what I actually wanted, what I could handle, whether I could do this alone. And during that period, the people around me... they stayed pretty much the same, honestly. My mom was there. My friends didn't really know yet. The father and I were figuring it out in this weird, painful dance.

*rubs face*

But there was no build-up period, no "trying" phase where relationships were tested over months or years. It was just this sudden shock, and then I had to make a decision, and then I had to tell people. So the relationships that mattered most were just... already there, or not.

*looks at you*

Does that make sense? I feel like maybe I'm not answering what you're actually trying to understand.
Tell me about the midwives, doctors, and other healthcare professionals who were part of your pregnancy care. Which relationships felt most important to you, and why?

*leans forward slightly, eyes brightening a bit*

Okay, so I've only had a few appointments so far, but... there's this midwife I saw last month who actually made me feel less crazy. Her name is Sarah, I think? She didn't treat me like I was just another data point on a chart.

*voice becomes more animated*

When I came in all nervous and using all the clinical terminology — you know, trying to sound like I had it together — she just... stopped me. She was like, "You can talk to me like a normal person." And something about that gave me permission to actually admit how scared I was. We talked about the fatigue, the anxiety, the weird body stuff that I was too embarrassed to mention to anyone else.

*pauses, expression becoming more guarded*

My actual doctor feels more distant. He's fine, competent, but he rushes through appointments. I get maybe ten minutes, and he doesn't really ask follow-up questions. Last time I tried to talk about my anxiety about miscarriage — which is, like, a legitimate concern in the first trimester — and he just said "statistically you're fine" and moved on.

*voice gets quieter*

That actually made me feel worse, not better. Like, I don't need statistics. I need someone to acknowledge that I'm terrified, even if the numbers say everything's okay.

*looks directly at you*

Sarah made me feel seen. And right now, that matters more than anything else. She's becoming the person I actually trust in all of this.
Who else was involved in your care that you'd like to mention — community midwives, consultants, support workers, interpreters?

*shakes head*

Not really, it's just been those two so far — Sarah the midwife and my doctor. I haven't seen any consultants or anything because, like I said, I'm "low risk," so there's no reason to, I guess.

*fidgets with jacket*

There's no support worker assigned to me or anything. I mean, I work in education, so I'm used to being independent and figuring things out on my own. But honestly? I think I could use someone. Like, not because there's anything medically wrong, but just... someone to help me navigate all of this emotionally and logistically.

*voice becomes a bit uncertain*

I don't even know if that's a thing that exists for people like me. Single, healthy, young. It feels like the system is set up for either high-risk pregnancies that need specialists, or women with partners who can advocate for them. I'm kind of in this weird middle space where nobody's really checking in on how I'm actually doing.

*looks down*

No interpreter needed — English is my first language — but sometimes I feel like I need someone to interpret what's happening in my own body, you know? To help me make sense of all the conflicting information I'm reading online versus what the providers are telling me.

*pauses*

Sarah's kind of filling that gap right now, but she's a midwife, not a support worker. I'm grateful she cares, but I also don't want to burden her with everything.
Tell me about your partner's — or your closest support person's — involvement during your pregnancy appointments and day-to-day experience. How did their presence or absence shape things for you?

*jaw tightens, eyes looking away*

He's not... he doesn't come to the appointments. That wasn't really discussed or planned, it just kind of happened that way.

*voice becomes quieter, more guarded*

I think part of me didn't want him there, honestly. Like, his involvement feels complicated and painful right now, and having him in those exam rooms would just make everything more awkward and real in a way I'm not ready for. So I've been going alone.

*pauses, fingers picking at a loose thread on my jacket*

The absence of him — of *anyone* — at those appointments is definitely something I feel. I sit in the waiting room and I watch other women come in with partners or mothers or friends, and there's this ache, you know? Like, I'm handling this on my own, which is fine, I can do it, but it doesn't mean I want to.

*voice cracks slightly*

Day-to-day, he texts occasionally asking how I'm doing, if the baby's okay. But it feels obligatory. We're not really *talking* about what this means or what happens next. And I can't really lean on him for support because the whole situation is wrapped up in this relationship that didn't work out.

*looks up at you*

So my closest support person is really my mom, but even that feels different than having a partner who's equally invested in this. She's being supportive, but it's not the same as having someone who's *choosing* to be here.

*voice becomes smaller*

I'm doing this pretty much alone.
How did that affect your relationship with each other — did pregnancy bring you closer together or create tensions?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes getting glossy*

It... it created a lot of tension, honestly.

*pauses, struggling with words*

When I first told him, he was scared. And I get that. But his fear came across as resentment, like I'd done this *to* him somehow. Even though we were both there, you know? Both responsible for what happened. But somehow it felt like he was treating me like I'd made a unilateral decision to get pregnant just to complicate his life.

*voice becomes harder*

There were conversations — if you can even call them that — where he kept asking if I was "sure" about going through with it. And I couldn't tell if he was genuinely worried about me, or if he was hoping I'd change my mind. It felt like pressure disguised as concern.

*wipes eyes quickly*

We weren't close before, not really. We were... I don't know, casual? Figuring things out? And this just blew everything up. Instead of bringing us together, it pushed us further apart because now there's this huge thing between us and we don't know how to talk about it without getting defensive.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

I think we're both grieving, in different ways. He's grieving the freedom he thought he had. I'm grieving the relationship we might have had if this hadn't happened. And the baby is just... there in the middle of all that mess.

*looks down*

We're distant now. Polite. But distant.

Tell me about the people — friends, family, online communities — who you turned to most during your pregnancy when you had questions or worries. How did those relationships work alongside your formal care?

*shifts uncomfortably*

Honestly? I haven't really turned to many people. That's kind of the problem.

*pauses, thinking*

My mom is the main one. When I'm panicking about something — like, is this amount of nausea normal, or should I be worried — I'll text her. She tries to be reassuring, but sometimes I can tell she's just Googling things too, which doesn't help either of us. We're both kind of in the dark together.

*rubs face*

I have a couple close friends who know, but I don't want to burden them with every worry and question. Like, I'll mention something casually, but I don't go to them with the deep stuff. And honestly, none of them have been pregnant, so they can't really relate. Their advice feels distant, well-meaning but ultimately not that helpful.

*voice becomes more hesitant*

Online communities... yeah, I've been lurking in some pregnancy forums and Reddit threads. It's a weird thing because I can be anonymous, so I can ask the embarrassing questions or admit how scared I am without anyone knowing. But it's also terrifying because you read horror stories and worst-case scenarios and then you spiral.

*looks down*

The thing is, those online people and my mom — they're not really working *alongside* my formal care. It's more like they're filling the gaps that formal care is leaving. Like, my doctor says "you're fine," but I still need someone to actually listen to my anxiety about it, you know?

*pauses*

Sarah, the midwife, is the only one who's actually bridging both worlds for me.

Tell me about a time during your pregnancy when you had to make a significant decision about a test, a procedure, or a care option. Who was involved in that decision with you, and how did each person's input land?

*takes a deep breath, becoming more serious*

The first big one was... deciding to actually go through with the pregnancy. That was the decision, right? Not a medical procedure, but still huge.

*looks away*

I had to decide alone, essentially. The father made it clear he was scared and unsure, which felt like pressure to reconsider. My mom said whatever I decided, she'd support me. But that put the weight entirely on me — like, I had to be the one to decide for all three of us, in a way.

*voice becomes quieter*

I didn't really have a healthcare provider guiding me through that decision either. I hadn't had my first prenatal appointment yet. So I was just... sitting with it, thinking about my life, my career, what I could actually handle. It was lonely and terrifying and I cried a lot.

*pauses*

Since then, there haven't been major decisions yet, thankfully. I'm only ten weeks, so we're past the point where I'm deciding whether to continue the pregnancy, but we haven't gotten to the big screening tests or anything like that.

*looks up with some anxiety in her eyes*

I'm actually kind of dreading that, to be honest. When I have to decide about the anatomy scan or genetic testing or all of that. Because I know I'll want someone there — someone knowledgeable — to help me understand what the options mean. And right now, I'm not sure who that person will be.

*voice wavers slightly*

I'm hoping it'll be Sarah.

Tell me about how your relationships with people outside the healthcare system — your employer, your community, your cultural or faith community — shaped your pregnancy experience. Who was supportive, who added pressure?

*shifts in seat, becoming more thoughtful*

Okay, so work has been... complicated. I'm an educator, right? And I haven't told my employer yet because I'm still figuring out my employment situation anyway. I've been looking for full-time positions, and finding out I'm pregnant kind of threw a wrench in that whole plan.

*voice becomes tense*

I keep thinking about how to navigate it. Like, do I tell a potential employer before I'm hired? After? It feels like it could affect whether they hire me, even though legally it shouldn't. So there's this anxiety around it that's separate from just being pregnant.

*pauses*

Community-wise, I'm not super plugged into anything religious or faith-based. My family's not particularly religious. So that's not really a source of support or pressure for me, which honestly might be a blessing because I know that can get complicated.

*voice becomes quieter*

The cultural thing... I don't think there's huge cultural pressure on me specifically, but being a single mother at twenty-three, especially in a place like Lynn where people kind of know each other? There's definitely an awareness of judgment, even if it's not directly in my face. Like, I feel it in the way some people might react when they find out.

*looks down*

My mom's friends have been supportive, which is nice. But it's her support network, not really mine. I feel kind of isolated in that way — like I don't have my own community holding me through this.

*meets your eyes*

That's been harder than I expected.
How did that affect your sense of identity — were there moments when being pregnant felt in tension with other parts of who you are?

*nods slowly, eyes getting emotional*

Yeah. All the time, actually.

*voice becomes strained*

I'm an educator. That's how I see myself — someone who's knowledgeable, in control, teaching others. And then I found out I was pregnant and suddenly I feel like a cautionary tale instead of an expert. Like, all those lectures I gave about responsible sexual health and planning? I'm living proof of what happens when things go wrong.

*fidgets with hands*

There's this weird disconnect where I know the science inside and out, but I can't apply it to myself without falling apart. I'm supposed to be the one with answers, and instead I'm the one frantically Googling at midnight wondering if cramping is normal.

*pauses, voice becoming quieter*

And career-wise, I was just starting to figure out my next steps — looking for full-time positions, thinking about my future. Now there's this baby coming and I have to rethink everything. Like, who am I if I'm not advancing professionally? Who am I as a mother when I haven't even figured out who I am as an adult yet?

*looks up, eyes glistening*

Some days I feel like I'm supposed to be grateful and glowing, and other days I'm grieving the version of myself that didn't have to make these choices. The independent educator Deeanna who could just... be.

*voice cracks*

I don't know how to be both. I don't know who I'm becoming, and that scares me more than any medical complication could.

Tell me about the moments in your pregnancy when your body was being examined or discussed by healthcare professionals. Who was present during those interactions, and how did their presence affect how you felt?

*becomes visibly uncomfortable, crossing arms*

It's... it's been weird being alone in those exam rooms, honestly. Like, when the doctor does the pelvic exam or the ultrasound, there's just me and him and a nurse sometimes. And I'm trying to stay professional, trying not to let on how vulnerable I feel lying there half-naked while someone's looking inside me.

*voice becomes quieter*

With Sarah, the midwife, it felt different. She explained what she was doing before she did it, and she checked in with me — like, "Is this okay? Are you comfortable?" It made me feel less like an object being prodded and more like a person making decisions about my own body.

*pauses, fidgeting*

But mostly I've been alone. No partner, no mom, no one. And I think that's made it harder, actually. Like, if someone I trusted was there, maybe I'd feel less exposed? Or more empowered to speak up if something felt wrong or uncomfortable?

*looks down*

There was one moment during my doctor's appointment where he was explaining something about my cervix or whatever, and he used language that made me feel... clinical. Like I was a case study rather than a person. And I wanted to say something, but I didn't because I was alone and I didn't want to seem difficult or ungrateful.

*voice becomes smaller*

I think having someone there — someone who knew me, who cared about me — would have changed that. I would've felt braver about advocating for myself.

*meets your eyes briefly*

Being examined alone is lonely.

Tell me about who was involved in helping you navigate the maternity system — booking appointments, understanding your options, knowing your rights. Who helped you find your way, and who made it harder?

*lets out a frustrated laugh*

Honestly? Nobody really helped me navigate it. I kind of just... figured it out as I went along.

*rubs forehead*

I found out I was pregnant, and then I had to Google where to go for prenatal care. I called my GP's office, they referred me to the midwifery clinic, and I booked an appointment. It was all very... transactional. No one sat down and explained the system to me or walked me through what to expect.

*voice becomes more frustrated*

I didn't really know what my options were. Like, I know there are different models of care — midwife-led, doctor-led, hospital versus birthing center — but nobody actually presented those to me as choices. It just kind of happened that I got assigned to this clinic and these providers.

*pauses*

My mom tried to help a little. She asked questions about what I'd be screened for, what the timeline looked like. But she didn't really know either, so we were both kind of guessing. And I was too embarrassed to admit I didn't understand a lot of the terminology in the paperwork they sent me, even though I *should* understand it given my background.

*voice becomes quieter*

Sarah has actually been the one helping me most. She's explained things in a way that makes sense, answered questions I didn't even know I had. But that's happened kind of organically through our conversations, not through any formal orientation to the system.

*looks at you*

I feel like I've been fumbling through this alone when someone could have actually guided me.
Who else was involved in translating the system for you — a friend who had been through it, an online group, a community health worker?

*shakes head slowly*

Not really anyone, honestly. And that's been part of the problem.

*leans back*

I have friends who've had babies, but like I said, they're not close friends — not people I felt comfortable asking "so what was your prenatal care like?" or "what should I expect?" It felt too personal to go there with them, especially when they don't know the full situation with me being single.

*voice becomes more uncertain*

Online, I've been reading forums and blogs, trying to piece things together myself. But that's actually made things more confusing because everyone's experience is different. One person will say "my midwife was amazing," another will say "I felt unheard," and I'm just sitting there wondering what my actual experience should be or what red flags to watch for.

*pauses*

I think there's an assumption that because I'm educated, because I work in health education, that I don't need help navigating the system. Like, surely I can figure it out. But knowing the biology of pregnancy and actually navigating maternity care are two completely different things. The system itself is confusing — insurance stuff, what different tests mean, when to call versus when to wait.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I wish there had been someone — like a community health worker or even just a peer who'd been through it recently — who could have said, "Here's how this actually works, here are your real options, here's what you should advocate for."

*looks at you*

I've been doing it blind.

When you first started thinking about having a baby, were there particular people — a midwife, GP, or someone else — whose opinion you felt you could really rely on? What made you feel you could trust them?

*pauses, looking confused*

I mean... I didn't really start thinking about having a baby. That's what I keep trying to say. This wasn't a journey where I was building trust with healthcare providers ahead of time because I wasn't planning for this.

*shifts in seat, a bit defensively*

Before I got pregnant, I had a GP I saw occasionally, but we didn't have any kind of deep relationship. I wasn't asking her about pregnancy planning or anything like that. So there was no one whose opinion I'd already learned to rely on.

*voice softens slightly*

The trust I'm building now is happening *after* the fact, which is harder. Like, Sarah the midwife — I'm learning to trust her because she listens to me and explains things clearly and doesn't make me feel judged. But that's something we're developing in real time, not something I came into this pregnancy with.

*looks down*

I think that's actually part of why this feels so isolating. Most women probably have these relationships established before they get pregnant. They know who they can call with questions, who they trust with their care. I'm trying to build that trust while simultaneously being terrified and hormonal and making huge life decisions.

*meets your eyes*

It's like I'm learning to swim while I'm already in the water, you know? I didn't have the foundation. And sometimes I wonder if that's made me more vulnerable to just accepting whatever care I'm given, because I don't have anyone to compare it to or validate whether it's good care.

Thinking back to conversations you had with healthcare professionals before you became pregnant — whether about timing, fertility, or your overall health — did you ever feel like decisions were being made for you rather than with you?

*pauses, thinking back*

I didn't really have those conversations before I got pregnant, so there's not much to look back on, honestly.

*voice becomes more thoughtful*

But I guess what I'm realizing now is that there *should* have been conversations. Like, when I was on birth control, my GP never sat down with me and talked through my options, what would happen if it failed, what my actual choices would be if I got pregnant unexpectedly. It was just... here's your prescription, come back in a year.

*fidgets with hands*

So in a way, decisions were being made for me by omission, if that makes sense? Like, the healthcare system had this assumption that I was a young, educated woman who had it all figured out. Nobody checked in about whether I actually understood the risks or what I'd do in different scenarios.

*voice becomes quieter*

And now that I'm pregnant, I'm noticing it happening again — like, things are being decided based on the fact that I'm "low risk" without really asking me what *I* want or what matters to me. My doctor just kind of... moves through the checklist without really asking my input.

*looks up*

I think if someone had actually talked *with* me before all this — not at me, with me — I might feel less like I'm being swept along by a system that doesn't really see me as a person making choices. I'd feel more in control of my own care.

*pauses*

Does that count as what you're asking?
Can you tell me a bit more about that moment? Who else was in the room, and how did it feel?

*looks a bit puzzled*

Which moment are you talking about? There have been a few different things...

*pauses, trying to clarify*

Are you asking about a specific appointment with my doctor, or...? Because honestly, most of them feel kind of the same. I go in, he checks me, asks a few quick questions, and then I'm out the door in like ten minutes.

*fidgets uncertainly*

Or are you asking about before I got pregnant? Because like I said, I didn't really have meaningful healthcare conversations back then. It was just routine stuff — annual physical, get my prescription refilled.

*voice becomes a bit defensive*

I feel like maybe I'm not explaining things clearly, or you're picking up on something specific that I said. I'm trying to be honest about my experience, but I also don't want to make it sound more dramatic than it is, you know?

*looks directly at you*

Can you help me understand what you're asking about? What moment are you wanting me to go back to? Because I want to give you the real answer, not just... I don't know, fill in details that didn't actually happen.

*leans forward slightly*

I'm trying to be truthful here. It's just a lot to untangle.

Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body — particularly in relation to the idea of pregnancy and everything that comes with it? Was that something you talked about openly with anyone?

*takes a moment, looking away*

That's... that's a good question, actually.

*fidgets with jacket sleeve*

I think I was pretty detached from it, honestly. My body was just my body — it worked, it did what I needed it to do. I didn't think about pregnancy much because it wasn't something I was planning for. And when I did think about it in an abstract way, it was always "someday, maybe, in the future when I'm ready."

*voice becomes quieter*

I didn't really talk about it openly with anyone. My mom and I aren't super close about physical stuff like that. And with friends, we'd joke about it sometimes — like, "oh God, I'm not ready for kids yet" — but we never had deep conversations about how we actually *felt* about our bodies or pregnancy.

*pauses, becoming more vulnerable*

I think part of me was kind of... afraid of my body, if I'm being honest. Not in a traumatic way, but like, I knew pregnancy would change things fundamentally and that felt scary. So I just didn't think about it.

*looks down*

And now my body is doing this thing I didn't prepare for mentally or emotionally. It's being hijacked by hormones, by another person's DNA. And I'm grieving the version of myself that could just exist without all these complications.

*voice cracks slightly*

I wish I'd actually thought about this stuff before. Talked to someone about what it would mean for me, not just biologically but... emotionally. Who I'd be.
When you were searching online or using apps for information before pregnancy, how did you decide what to trust? Were there sources that felt reliable to you, and others that didn't?

*shifts uncomfortably*

Well, I wasn't really searching online for pregnancy information before I got pregnant, because like I said, I wasn't thinking about having a baby. So there wasn't this deliberate process of figuring out what to trust.

*pauses*

But now that I'm pregnant? Oh man, it's a minefield. I've downloaded a couple apps — What to Expect, one of those — and I'll be honest, sometimes they're helpful and sometimes they send me into a spiral. Like, they'll have a symptom tracker where you can log things, and then you see all these comments from other women and suddenly you're reading about rare complications at 2 AM.

*voice becomes more frustrated*

With my education background, I *should* be able to critically evaluate sources, right? I know to look for peer-reviewed stuff, reputable organizations. And I do try — I'll check NHS websites, things like that. But the problem is that doesn't really calm my anxiety. Like, even if I know statistically I'm fine, I'm still *feeling* terrified.

*rubs face*

Reddit threads feel more honest than clinical websites, but they're also scarier because people tend to post about their problems more than their normal experiences. So I get this skewed view of what pregnancy is actually like.

*looks at you*

I think what I'm realizing is that I can't think my way out of this anxiety with better sources. I need someone to actually talk to me about it, not just give me information. And that's something the apps and websites can't do.

Before you became pregnant, were there things about your body — your weight, a health condition, your age, or anything else — that felt like they might be seen as a problem or a risk by healthcare professionals? How did that feel?

*becomes more thoughtful, less defensive*

You know, I'm twenty-three, so age-wise I'm actually in a good window for pregnancy. No one's treating me like I'm too young or too old. So that's not really a thing.

*pauses, considering*

Health-wise, I don't have any chronic conditions or anything like that. I'm generally healthy. So medically speaking, I check all the boxes for "low risk," which should be reassuring but somehow isn't.

*voice becomes quieter, more hesitant*

I guess the thing that feels like a problem is... being unmarried. Not medically, but socially, you know? Like, my body itself is fine, but the *circumstances* of my pregnancy — single, unplanned — that feels like something healthcare providers might judge me for, even if they don't say it out loud.

*fidgets with hands*

I remember that one nurse asking about my sexual health and I felt this flush of shame. Like, she was just doing her job, but I felt like she was silently judging me for not being more careful. And I was already judging myself, so her neutral professionalism somehow made it feel worse.

*looks down*

Before I got pregnant, I never really thought about my body as being "problematic" in a healthcare sense. But now I'm hyperaware that I'm a young, single woman having an unplanned pregnancy, and I wonder if that changes how seriously people take my concerns or how they perceive my decisions.

*voice wavers*

It's less about my actual body and more about the story attached to it.
Before you were pregnant, how involved was your partner — or someone close to you — in conversations about trying for a baby, including any appointments or decisions about your health?

*lets out a small, sad laugh*

He wasn't involved at all, because we weren't trying. There was nothing to be involved in.

*voice becomes quieter, more guarded*

I mean, we were together — casually — but we weren't having conversations about the future or babies or any of that. It was just... what it was. So there were no appointments he came to, no health decisions we made together about pregnancy planning.

*pauses, looking uncomfortable*

I think that's actually part of why this is so hard now. If we'd had some kind of relationship where we were talking about bigger things, maybe this wouldn't feel so isolating. But we didn't have that foundation. So when I got pregnant, it wasn't this thing we could navigate together. It was something that happened *to* both of us separately.

*voice becomes more strained*

And now, trying to involve him in decisions after the fact feels awkward and painful. Like, I'm asking him to suddenly be invested in something he never agreed to, and he's dealing with his own shock and fear. We're both kind of fumbling.

*looks down*

If someone had actually talked to me about relationship health and communication before I got pregnant — like, what would happen if contraception failed, how would we handle it — maybe I'd be in a different position now. But I was just living my life, not thinking about contingencies.

*meets your eyes briefly*

Nobody prepares you for that conversation, I guess.
Did your partner come to any consultations with you, and if so, how did that change the dynamic in those appointments?

*shakes head firmly*

No, he hasn't come to any appointments. Like I said, we decided — or I decided, really — that he wouldn't be there.

*voice becomes more defensive*

I think part of me was protective, honestly. Like, if he came to the appointments, it would make everything feel more real and final for both of us. And I wasn't sure where he stood, whether he actually wanted to be involved or if he was just... going through the motions because he felt obligated.

*pauses, looking conflicted*

Plus, having him there would've made the appointments about *us* instead of about *me* and my care. And right now, I need to focus on what's happening to my body, what I need. His presence would've been distracting in a way that didn't feel helpful.

*voice becomes quieter*

We've talked about him coming to future appointments — like, maybe the anatomy scan or something — but I haven't committed to it. Part of me thinks it would be good for him to see the baby, to feel more connected. But another part of me is still hurt and angry, and I'm not sure I want him there witnessing all of this.

*fidgets with hands*

So no, he hasn't come. And honestly? I'm not sure if that's been better or worse for my care. It's made me feel more alone, but it's also meant I can focus on myself without having to manage his emotions too.

*looks up*

Maybe that will change as things progress.

Before you became pregnant, were there moments in your interactions with the healthcare system where you felt like your voice mattered — and moments where it really didn't? I'd love to hear about both, if you're comfortable sharing.

*takes a deep breath, thinking back carefully*

Honestly? I don't think I had enough interactions with healthcare before this to really form strong opinions either way. I wasn't someone who went to the doctor regularly or had ongoing relationships with providers.

*voice becomes more measured*

When I did go in for my annual physical or whatever, the appointments felt pretty transactional. The nurse would check my vitals, ask some standard questions, and then the doctor would come in for maybe five minutes. I don't remember feeling like my voice particularly *mattered*, but I also didn't feel like it didn't, you know? It was just... clinical and efficient.

*pauses*

I think the moment that stuck with me was asking about birth control options and the nurse seeming kind of dismissive, like I should already know what I wanted. But I didn't push back on it because I was young and didn't know I *could* push back.

*voice becomes more present*

What I'm noticing now is that I'm *looking* for moments where my voice matters, because I need it to. With Sarah, I feel heard. With my doctor, I feel like a checkbox. And that difference is so stark that I'm acutely aware of it.

*looks at you*

I think before I got pregnant, I just accepted healthcare as it was. I didn't know enough to demand better. Now that I'm actually vulnerable and scared, suddenly it matters so much more that someone listens to me.

*pauses*

Does that make sense?

As your pregnancy progressed, how did your sense of what you wanted from your care change — particularly around having your own wishes respected and feeling like you were genuinely in charge of decisions about your body?

*leans back, considering the question carefully*

I mean, I'm only ten weeks in, so it's still pretty early. But yeah, things have shifted already.

*voice becomes more thoughtful*

At first, when I found out I was pregnant, I was just in survival mode. Like, okay, this is happening, what do I do? I didn't really think about what I *wanted* from my care — I just wanted to make sure the baby was okay and figure out logistically what came next.

*pauses*

But as I've had appointments and started to settle into the reality of this, I'm realizing I care more about being treated like a person who gets to make choices. Not just being told what's "best" for me, but actually being asked what matters to me.

*voice becomes more animated*

Like, with Sarah, she asks questions. "How are you feeling about this? What are your concerns?" With my doctor, he just tells me what's happening next. And the difference is huge. It makes me feel like I have some control, even though objectively my body is doing all these things I didn't plan for.

*looks down*

I think as I go further, I'm going to want more say in things — what tests to do, how I want to approach labor, all of that. I don't want to just be a vessel that medical professionals manage. I want to be *in charge* of decisions about my own body, even if I need their expertise to understand the options.

*voice wavers slightly*

I'm still learning what that actually looks like, though.
Were there moments when you felt truly listened to and respected as the expert on your own experience — and moments when you didn't?

*nods slowly, eyes becoming more emotional*

Yeah, definitely both.

*voice becomes warmer*

With Sarah, there was this moment where I was describing my fatigue and anxiety, kind of apologizing for "complaining" about it, and she just... stopped me. She said, "You're the expert on your body. If something feels wrong to you, that matters. I trust what you're telling me." 

*pauses, getting a bit teary*

That was huge. Because I've been second-guessing myself constantly — like, am I overreacting? Is this normal? And having someone say that my experience is valid, that I know my own body... it made me feel less crazy.

*voice becomes harder*

But with my doctor, it's the opposite. I tried to talk about anxiety during my last appointment — like, legitimate worry about miscarriage in the first trimester — and he just rattled off statistics. "Your risk is low, statistically you're fine." And I'm sitting there thinking, but I don't *feel* fine. My body is screaming at me that something could go wrong.

*looks directly at you*

He didn't acknowledge that my fear was real, even if the statistics say I'm unlikely to miscarry. He acted like the numbers should be enough to make me stop worrying. But that's not how anxiety works.

*voice becomes quieter*

I felt like he was dismissing my experience instead of hearing it. Like, I'm not the expert on my own body in his eyes — the data is.

*pauses*

And that makes me trust him less, honestly.
During your pregnancy, how involved was your partner — or anyone else close to you — in decisions about your care, appointments, or birth plan? How did you feel about that level of involvement?

*shakes head*

He's not involved in any of that, really. We haven't talked about birth plans or anything like that. It's too early for most of those conversations anyway, I think.

*voice becomes uncertain*

I mean, I haven't even *had* a birth plan conversation with my healthcare providers yet, so it's definitely not something I've discussed with him. We're kind of just... existing separately in this situation right now.

*pauses, looking conflicted*

Part of me wishes he was more involved because then I wouldn't feel so alone in making all these decisions. But another part of me is protective of my autonomy, especially given how things are between us. Like, if he was pushing for certain things or trying to control decisions, that would feel worse than him just... not being there.

*fidgets with hands*

My mom knows when my appointments are, and I'll tell her how they went. But she's not coming to them either. So I'm making all these choices by myself — deciding what tests to do, what questions to ask — without anyone I'm close to actually present or involved in the process.

*voice becomes smaller*

I think as things progress, especially closer to labor, I might want someone there with me. Someone to advocate for me, to hold my hand. But right now, at ten weeks, it still feels manageable to do this alone.

*looks at you*

Though I'm not sure if that's healthy or just... what I've gotten used to.
Were there times when your partner's presence in appointments changed what was said — by you, or by the healthcare professionals?

*looks confused for a moment*

He hasn't been to any appointments, so... no, that hasn't happened.

*pauses, then adds more thoughtfully*

Though I guess I'm wondering sometimes if it *would* change things if he was there. Like, would I feel more confident speaking up? Or would I be too focused on managing his emotions to focus on my own care?

*fidgets*

I think about it sometimes — like, when my doctor is rushing through things or not really listening to me, would he say something if he was sitting there? Would he advocate for me? Or would he just sit there quietly like I do?

*voice becomes quieter*

Honestly, I don't know if having him there would make things better or worse. Part of me thinks having a partner present might make healthcare providers take you more seriously — like you're not just some single woman making decisions alone, you have someone backing you up. But that also feels kind of messed up, you know? Why should my voice matter more if there's a man in the room?

*looks down*

So no, there haven't been moments where his presence changed things, because he's not been present. But I do think about whether his absence has made me less likely to push back or ask questions. Like, maybe if I had someone there supporting me, I'd feel braver.

*meets your eyes*

I'll probably know more about this as my pregnancy goes on.

How did your relationship with your body change during pregnancy — did you feel more connected to it, or did it bring up feelings that were harder to sit with? Was there anyone you felt safe talking to about that?

*takes a shaky breath, becoming more vulnerable*

It's... it's been really strange, honestly. My body feels like it's not entirely mine anymore, and that's been hard to process.

*voice becomes quieter*

The physical stuff — the nausea, the fatigue, the way my breasts are tender — it's all this constant reminder that something is happening that I didn't choose. And intellectually, I know it's miraculous and all that, but emotionally? Sometimes it just feels invasive. Like my body has been commandeered by these hormones and this pregnancy.

*pauses, looking down*

I haven't really felt more connected to my body. If anything, I feel more disconnected. Like I'm watching it change from the outside, not inhabiting it. Does that sound weird?

*voice becomes even quieter*

And I haven't talked to anyone about this part. My mom asked how I'm feeling physically, but when I try to explain the weirdness of it — the alienation — I don't have the words. It sounds ungrateful if I say "my body feels like it's not mine," you know?

*looks up with glistening eyes*

Sarah actually asked me about it once, in a different way. She asked how I was adjusting emotionally, and I started to open up about feeling disconnected. And she didn't make me feel broken for feeling that way. But even with her, I've only scratched the surface.

*voice wavers*

I think I'm grieving the version of my body that was just... mine. And I don't know who to talk to about that grief.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*pauses, thinking carefully*

Yeah, actually. There's something that's been sitting with me.

*voice becomes more urgent*

I think what's been hardest about all of this isn't really the medical stuff or even the physical changes. It's the loneliness of making this journey without anyone who's equally invested in it. Like, I have people who care about me — my mom, my friends — but this is fundamentally *my* experience. My body, my life changing, my future being reshaped.

*looks directly at you*

And I think healthcare providers need to understand that. Not everyone comes in with a partner or a strong support system. Some of us are doing this alone, and that doesn't make us less deserving of care that actually *sees* us and listens to us.

*voice becomes more emotional*

Sarah does that for me. But I got lucky finding her. What if I hadn't? What if my only provider had been my doctor who treats me like a checkbox?

*pauses*

I also wish someone had talked to me — before this happened, or even now — about the emotional side of an unplanned pregnancy. Not just "congratulations, here's your prenatal vitamins," but actually acknowledging that this is grief and joy and terror all mixed together. That it's okay to feel ambivalent about something that's biologically miraculous.

*voice wavers slightly*

And maybe... maybe if the system had made space for that conversation earlier, I wouldn't feel like such an imposter trying to become a mother.
